title: Ἀλώπηξ καὶ βότρυς
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 32 (= Perry 15). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« »). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Ἀλώπηξ λιμώττουσα, ὡς ἐθεάσατο ἀπό τινος ἀναδενδράδος βότρυας κρεμαμένους, ἠβουλήθη αὐτῶν περιγενέσθαι καὶ οὐκ ἠδύνατο.
sentences.tr: Alōpēx limōttousa, hōs etheasato apo tinos anadendrados botryas kremamenous, ēboulēthē autōn perigenesthai kai ouk ēdynato.
sentences.l: A fox, famishing, when she beheld, from a certain tree-trained vine, grape-clusters hanging, wished to get-the-better of them — and could not.
sentences.i: A starving fox saw bunches of grapes hanging from a vine trained up a tree, and longed to get at them, but could not.
sentences.n: The Aesopic engine in one sentence: a present participle (λιμώττουσα) sets the state, aorists (ἐθεάσατο, ἠβουλήθη) fire the events — participle-plus-verb chains carry these fables; you'll see this constantly. ἀναδενδράς is a vine trained up a tree (ἀνά 'up' + δένδρον 'tree'). περιγενέσθαι takes the genitive: the grapes are an opponent to 'get the better of.'

sentences.t: Ἀπαλλαττομένη δὲ πρὸς ἑαυτὴν εἶπεν· « Ὄμφακές εἰσιν. »
sentences.tr: Apallattomenē de pros heautēn eipen: « Omphakes eisin. »
sentences.l: And going-away she said to herself: « Unripe-grapes they are. »
sentences.i: As she went off she said to herself, « They're sour grapes. »
sentences.n: The whole idiom 'sour grapes' is these two words: Ὄμφακές εἰσιν. An ὄμφαξ is an unripe — therefore sour — grape. The raised dot · is the Greek semicolon/colon; the guillemets « » are Chambry's editorial quotation marks, kept as printed. Ὄμφακές carries a second accent thrown back from the enclitic εἰσιν.

sentences.t: Οὕτω καὶ τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἔνιοι τῶν πραγμάτων ἐφικέσθαι μὴ δυνάμενοι δι' ἀσθένειαν τοὺς καιροὺς αἰτιῶνται.
sentences.tr: Houtō kai tōn anthrōpōn enioi tōn pragmatōn ephikesthai mē dynamenoi di' astheneian tous kairous aitiōntai.
sentences.l: So also of men some, the things to-attain not being-able through weakness, the circumstances blame.
sentences.i: So too some men, unable through their own weakness to attain their ends, blame circumstances.
sentences.n: The epimythium — the moral attached to a fable. This one opens Οὕτω καί, 'so also…', mapping the story onto life; most fables instead use the formula ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι, 'the story shows that…'. Notice the verb αἰτιῶνται held to the very end, after everything the men cannot do.
